lessons.name: OpenAI — what their values look like in interviews
Anthropic — values and how they probe in interviews
Palantir — values, with a focus on failure stories

cards.q: STAR weighting for Forward Deployed Engineer (FDE) behavioral rounds?
cards.a: Situation: 2-3 sentences MAX. Task: clarify YOUR ownership (FDE rounds probe individual accountability). Action: ~60% of the story, focus on technical + customer decisions you made. Result: both technical outcome and quantified business impact.

cards.q: Five must-have stories for a Forward Deployed Engineer (FDE) loop?
cards.a: (1) Production fix under live customer pressure. (2) Pushing back on a client request. (3) Owning a deployment failure end-to-end. (4) Explaining a hard technical limit to a non-technical stakeholder. (5) Making a decision with incomplete information.

cards.q: Palantir failure-story bar?
cards.a: Real, specific, owned, with the actual mechanism + cost + systemic change you made afterward. "I work too hard" / "I helped with..." are instant-reject signals.

cards.q: OpenAI Charter principles and 2025/2026 context to know for an interview?
cards.a: Four Charter principles: Broadly Distributed Benefits, Long-Term Safety, Technical Leadership, Cooperative Orientation. As of Oct 2025, OpenAI restructured: the for-profit is now OpenAI Group PBC under the OpenAI Foundation (nonprofit). Have a specific reason for the Forward Deployed Engineer (FDE) role, not just 'OpenAI'.

cards.q: Anthropic values to know?
cards.a: AI safety, beneficial AI, Constitutional AI, and the Responsible Scaling Policy (RSP v3.0, Feb 2026) — which moved to a two-track model (Anthropic-only mitigations + industry-wide map) and added a public Frontier Safety Roadmap. Expect ethical-dilemma scenarios; surface depth on safety, not generic concern.

cards.q: 'Why are you leaving your current role?' -- strongest framing?
cards.a: Pull-not-push: lead with what attracts you to the new role, keep current employer light-positive. Badmouthing signals you will do the same to the next employer in a year.

cards.q: 'Disagree and commit' -- what half do most candidates skip?
cards.a: The COMMIT half. A story where you disagreed AND won is incomplete. Stronger arc: disagreed, brought data, lost, executed fully, validated the team's view in hindsight.

cards.q: 5 required Forward Deployed Engineer (FDE) stories?
cards.a: (1) Production fix under pressure. (2) Pushing back on a customer. (3) Deployment failure ownership. (4) Explaining a technical limit. (5) Decision under incomplete info.

cards.q: Biggest behavioral red flags in a Palantir interview?
cards.a: (1) Fake-failure dodges ('I work too hard', 'I don't really fail'). (2) Surface-level 'why Palantir' — citing comp/perks/'cool problems' instead of specifics. (3) Mixing up Foundry (commercial) and Gotham (gov). (4) Inability to justify a technical choice. Cultural-fit signals weigh heavily; they reject strong technical candidates over these.

cards.q: Pull-vs-push framing for 'why are you leaving?'
cards.a: Pull: name the specific role/scope/mission attracting you. Push (badmouthing current employer) is the trap -- interviewers read it as a predictor of how you will describe them next year.

cards.q: STAR weighting for FDE — biggest section?
cards.a: Action ~60% of the story. Be specific about both technical and interpersonal moves you made.

cards.q: 5 required FDE stories?
cards.a: (1) Production fix under pressure. (2) Pushing back on customer. (3) Deployment failure ownership. (4) Explaining a technical limit. (5) Decision under incomplete info.

cards.q: OpenAI values cues for interviews (2026)?
cards.a: Charter still anchors on AGI that benefits humanity, now operationalized into five principles: Democratization, Empowerment, Universal Prosperity, Resilience, Adaptability. Culture rewards intensity, scale, and shipping products users love. Bring a role-specific reason, not a mission quote.

cards.q: Anthropic interview signal markers (2026)?
cards.a: Show real engagement with safety: RSP v3.0 (Feb 2026 -- replaced hard pauses with a public Frontier Safety Roadmap plus recurring risk assessments), Constitutional AI, and the Acceptable Use Policy. Generic 'I do the right thing' answers fail ethical-dilemma probes.

cards.q: "Tell me about a time you disagreed with your manager" — what most candidates skip?
cards.a: The COMMIT half: executing fully on the team's decision, even when you lost. Often stronger when you were partly wrong + did your share.

cards.q: "Tell me about mentoring someone" — what makes it strong?
cards.a: Specific person + specific gap + your approach + measurable outcome (promotion, project shipped, observable behavior change).

cards.q: In STAR, what fraction of a strong answer should be Action?
cards.a: Roughly 60%. Situation and Task together are ~20% (2-3 sentences of context, plus a one-line scope of YOUR ownership), Action is ~60% (specific technical and interpersonal moves you personally made), Result is ~20% (technical outcome + quantified business impact).

cards.q: Why is 'we' a red flag in behavioral answers, and how do you fix it mid-story?
cards.a: 'We' hides individual ownership -- the exact thing FDE rounds probe. Fix: switch to 'I' for every decision and action ('I escalated to the on-call', 'I wrote the rollback script'), and reserve 'we' only for true group context.

cards.q: What three elements separate a 'real failure' story from a sanitized one at Palantir?
cards.a: (1) A concrete mechanism -- what actually broke and why. (2) Real cost -- dollars, users, downtime, or trust lost. (3) A systemic change you made afterward (process, monitoring, or design) so it cannot recur. Without all three it reads as a humblebrag.

cards.q: Three instant-reject failure-story openers at Palantir?
cards.a: (1) 'I work too hard.' (2) 'I'm a perfectionist.' (3) 'I don't really fail / I can't think of one.' All signal lack of self-awareness or unwillingness to own a real mistake.

cards.q: What single detail most strengthens a 'production fix under pressure' story?
cards.a: A timestamped sequence with the specific decision points -- e.g., 'at T+4min I rolled back the deploy, at T+11min I patched the config, at T+23min traffic recovered'. Specificity proves you actually owned the incident rather than narrating someone else's.

cards.q: When pushing back on a customer request, what must the Action section include?
cards.a: The alternative you proposed and why it served the customer's underlying goal better -- not just 'I said no'. Strong stories show you preserved the relationship by reframing the ask, often with a smaller scoped pilot or a different mechanism that hit the same outcome.

cards.q: In a deployment-failure story, what distinguishes 'ownership' from 'involvement'?
cards.a: Ownership means you ran the rollback, the comms, AND the postmortem -- and the systemic fix shipped under your name. Involvement is 'I helped the on-call'. Interviewers listen for first-person verbs on every step of the recovery.

cards.q: How do you explain a hard technical limit to a non-technical stakeholder without sounding dismissive?
cards.a: Translate the constraint into their unit of value (revenue, latency for their users, regulatory risk), offer 2-3 ranked alternatives with tradeoffs, and let them choose. Never end with 'it's impossible' -- end with 'here are the options and what each costs'.

cards.q: What makes a 'decision under incomplete information' story strong vs weak?
cards.a: Strong: you name the specific unknowns, the cheapest experiment you ran to reduce them, your decision criteria, and what you would have done differently with hindsight. Weak: 'I went with my gut and it worked out' -- gives no signal on judgment.

cards.q: Why is 3-5 deep stories better prep than 20 shallow ones?
cards.a: Each deep story can be retold from multiple angles (failure, conflict, leadership, customer-first) to answer ~80% of behavioral prompts. Twenty shallow stories crumble under follow-up probing because you cannot recall mechanism, metric, or sequence.

cards.q: What are the four specificity hooks that make a behavioral answer feel real?
cards.a: (1) Named people or teams (anonymized if needed). (2) Dates or durations ('over six weeks', 'in Q3 2024'). (3) Quantified metrics (dollars, percent, users, latency). (4) The exact tool/system named ('Postgres replica', 'Terraform module').

cards.q: Customer-first vs engineering-pride -- how do you signal the right tradeoff?
cards.a: Tell a story where you shipped a less elegant solution because the customer needed it Friday, and you scheduled the refactor as a follow-up. The signal is that you optimized for customer outcome on the critical path, not for your own technical aesthetics.

cards.q: What is the strongest pattern for 'leading without authority'?
cards.a: Identify a cross-team gap nobody owned, write the doc/spec, get 2-3 key stakeholders to commit in 1:1s before any group meeting, then run the meeting against your draft. The Action is influence work (pre-wiring, written artifacts), not formal authority.

cards.q: How should you recover mid-interview after giving a weak behavioral answer?
cards.a: Name it: 'That answer rambled -- let me give you the 30-second version.' Then deliver tight STAR. Self-awareness plus correction is itself a positive signal; pretending it went well is worse than acknowledging.

cards.q: How do you tailor the same story for OpenAI vs Anthropic vs Palantir?
cards.a: Same arc, different emphasis: OpenAI -- shipping velocity and user impact at scale. Anthropic -- the safety/risk reasoning and tradeoffs you weighed. Palantir -- ownership, on-the-ground customer work, and explicit failure-mechanism honesty.

cards.q: Two Palantir-specific 'why us' traps to avoid?
cards.a: (1) Confusing Foundry (commercial data platform) with Gotham (government/intel) -- mixing them up signals you did not do basic research. (2) Citing comp, perks, or 'cool problems' instead of a specific product, customer segment, or mission angle you can defend with detail.
